answer:
Designing an HTTP Semantics Implementation

1. Definition & Context

HTTP semantics define the meaning of HTTP methods, status codes, headers, and content negotiation. Beyond basic GET/POST, intermediate mastery covers idempotency guarantees (GET, PUT, DELETE are idempotent; POST is not), conditional requests (ETag, If-None-Match, If-Modified-Since for cache validation), content negotiation (Accept, Content-Type headers), and proper status code usage (201 Created with Location header, 204 No Content, 409 Conflict, 422 Unprocessable Entity).

Understanding HTTP semantics prevents common API design mistakes: using POST for everything, returning 200 for errors, ignoring cache headers, and misusing 404 vs 410.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, the Fastify API followed strict HTTP semantics across 12+ REST endpoints. Creating a leave request returned 201 Created with a Location: /api/leaves/42 header. Updating leave status (approve/reject) used PATCH (partial update) returning 200 with the updated resource. Deleting a cancelled leave returned 204 No Content. When a manager tried to approve an already-processed leave, the API returned 409 Conflict with a descriptive error body. For the leave balance endpoint, Manoj implemented ETag-based caching: the balance rarely changed, so subsequent GET requests returned 304 Not Modified, reducing database queries by 60%.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// Fastify route with proper HTTP semantics (HR Leave System)
import { FastifyInstance } from 'fastify';
import { createHash } from 'crypto';

async function leaveRoutes(fastify: FastifyInstance) {
  // POST — 201 Created with Location header
  fastify.post('/api/leaves', async (request, reply) => {
    const leave = await prisma.leave.create({ data: request.body });
    reply
      .code(201)
      .header('Location', `/api/leaves/${leave.id}`)
      .send(leave);
  });

  // GET — ETag-based conditional response
  fastify.get('/api/leaves/balance/:userId', async (request, reply) => {
    const balance = await prisma.leaveBalance.findUnique({
      where: { userId: request.params.userId }
    });
    if (!balance) return reply.code(404).send({ error: 'User not found' });
    
    const etag = createHash('md5')
      .update(JSON.stringify(balance))
      .digest('hex');
    
    if (request.headers['if-none-match'] === etag) {
      return reply.code(304).send();
    }
    reply.header('ETag', etag).send(balance);
  });

  // PATCH — 409 Conflict for already-processed leave
  fastify.patch('/api/leaves/:id/status', async (request, reply) => {
    const leave = await prisma.leave.findUnique({ where: { id: request.params.id } });
    if (!leave) return reply.code(404).send({ error: 'Leave not found' });
    if (leave.status !== 'pending') {
      return reply.code(409).send({
        error: 'Conflict',
        message: `Leave already ${leave.status}`
      });
    }
    const updated = await prisma.leave.update({
      where: { id: request.params.id },
      data: { status: request.body.status }
    });
    reply.send(updated);
  });

  // DELETE — 204 No Content
  fastify.delete('/api/leaves/:id', async (request, reply) => {
    await prisma.leave.delete({ where: { id: request.params.id } });
    reply.code(204).send();
  });
}

5. Debugging Narrative — HR Leave System

Symptom: The Angular frontend's leave balance display showed stale data after a leave was approved. The balance number didn't update until the user hard-refreshed the page.

Investigation: Manoj checked the Network tab and saw the GET /api/leaves/balance request returning 304 Not Modified. The ETag hadn't changed even though the balance should have decreased.

Root Cause: The ETag was generated from the balance record, but the leave approval PATCH endpoint updated the leaves table without updating the leaveBalance record. The balance was computed in a separate cron job that ran every 5 minutes.

Fix: Manoj added a Prisma transaction that decremented the balance atomically within the leave approval endpoint. This ensured the ETag changed immediately after approval, and subsequent GET requests returned 200 with fresh data instead of 304.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Using 200 for all responses (even errors)Map domain outcomes to correct HTTP status codes
POST for read operations (breaks caching)Use GET for reads; POST only for creation
ETag mismatch after related data changesInclude all relevant data in ETag computation
Missing Content-Type on responsesConfigure Fastify serializers to set correct headers

7. Validation Strategy

  • API tests: Assert exact status codes and headers for each endpoint
  • Postman collection: Test conditional requests with ETag flow
  • Integration test: Verify 409 Conflict for concurrent status updates
  • Idempotency test: Call DELETE twice — first 204, second 404

Debugging HTTP Semantics Issues

1. Definition & Context

HTTP semantics debugging involves diagnosing issues where incorrect method usage, status codes, or header handling causes unexpected client behavior. Common bugs: CORS preflight failures (OPTIONS not handled), cache poisoning (wrong Cache-Control headers), idempotency violations (duplicate POST creating duplicates), and content type mismatches (sending JSON but Content-Type header says text/plain).

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, the bulk CSV import endpoint occasionally created duplicate contacts. Users uploaded the same CSV file twice because the first request timed out (the server was processing 500 records), and the retry created duplicates. The issue was a semantic problem: POST is not idempotent, so retrying it creates new resources.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// Idempotency key middleware for Fastify (ContactsHub)
const idempotencyStore = new Map<string, { status: number; body: any }>();

fastify.addHook('preHandler', async (request, reply) => {
  if (request.method !== 'POST') return; // Only non-idempotent methods
  
  const key = request.headers['x-idempotency-key'] as string;
  if (!key) return; // No key = normal behavior
  
  const cached = idempotencyStore.get(key);
  if (cached) {
    // Return cached response — prevents duplicate processing
    reply.code(cached.status).send(cached.body);
    return;
  }
});

fastify.addHook('onSend', async (request, reply, payload) => {
  if (request.method !== 'POST') return payload;
  const key = request.headers['x-idempotency-key'] as string;
  if (!key) return payload;
  
  idempotencyStore.set(key, {
    status: reply.statusCode,
    body: JSON.parse(payload as string)
  });
  // Clean up after 24 hours
  setTimeout(() => idempotencyStore.delete(key), 86400000);
  return payload;
});

// Client usage
const uploadCSV = async (file: File) => {
  const idempotencyKey = `csv-${file.name}-${file.size}-${file.lastModified}`;
  const response = await fetch('/api/contacts/import', {
    method: 'POST',
    headers: {
      'X-Idempotency-Key': idempotencyKey,
      'Content-Type': 'text/csv'
    },
    body: file
  });
  return response.json();
};

5. Debugging Narrative — ContactsHub

Symptom: After a large CSV upload, the contacts list showed 1000 entries instead of the expected 500. Users reported they only uploaded one file.

Investigation: Manoj checked the Fastify access logs and found two POST requests to /api/contacts/import within 5 seconds of each other, both with status 201. The client-side code used axios with a 30-second timeout, but the server took 35 seconds to process 500 records. Axios timed out and the React error boundary caught it, showing a retry button. The user clicked retry, sending the same CSV again.

Root Cause: No idempotency protection on the import endpoint. POST is inherently non-idempotent, so the server treated the retry as a new import.

Fix: Manoj added an idempotency key based on file name + size + lastModified timestamp. The server stored the key in a Redis cache (later simplified to in-memory Map for the small-scale app). On retry, the server returned the cached 201 response without re-processing. He also increased the client timeout to 120 seconds and added a progress indicator via Server-Sent Events.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Idempotency key not unique enoughInclude file hash or UUID generated client-side
CORS preflight fails for custom headersAdd X-Idempotency-Key to Access-Control-Allow-Headers
Cache-Control: no-store on mutable resourcesUse no-cache (revalidate) not no-store (never cache)
PUT used for partial updatesUse PATCH for partial; PUT replaces entire resource

7. Validation Strategy

  • Duplicate test: Send same POST twice with same idempotency key — assert only one resource created
  • Timeout test: Simulate slow server, verify client retry behavior
  • CORS test: Verify preflight OPTIONS returns correct allow-headers
  • Status code audit: Review all endpoints return semantically correct codes

Comparing REST Constraint Approaches

1. Definition & Context

REST constraints are the six architectural principles defined by Roy Fielding: client-server, statelessness, cacheability, uniform interface, layered system, and code-on-demand (optional). At the intermediate level, the practical debate centers on strict RESTful resource modeling (HATEOAS, hypermedia controls, resource-oriented URLs) versus pragmatic REST (RPC-style actions, query parameters for filtering, custom headers for pagination).

The uniform interface constraint has four sub-constraints: resource identification (URIs), resource manipulation through representations, self-descriptive messages, and HATEOAS (Hypermedia as the Engine of Application State). Most production APIs implement the first three but skip HATEOAS.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave System vs ContactsHub

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the API followed pragmatic REST: resources were /api/leaves, /api/users, /api/departments, but approval actions used a verb-style endpoint PATCH /api/leaves/:id/approve instead of the strictly RESTful PATCH /api/leaves/:id { status: 'approved' }. This pragmatic choice made the API self-documenting for the Angular frontend team. In contrast, ContactsHub followed stricter REST: PATCH /api/contacts/:id with a JSON body describing the state change, and pagination used Link headers (Link: </api/contacts?page=2>; rel="next") for discoverability.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Code Example

// Approach A: Strict REST with HATEOAS (ContactsHub)
// GET /api/contacts/42
{
  "id": 42,
  "name": "Ravi Kumar",
  "email": "ravi@example.com",
  "_links": {
    "self": { "href": "/api/contacts/42" },
    "edit": { "href": "/api/contacts/42", "method": "PATCH" },
    "delete": { "href": "/api/contacts/42", "method": "DELETE" },
    "avatar": { "href": "/api/contacts/42/avatar" }
  }
}

// Approach B: Pragmatic REST (HR Leave System)
// POST /api/leaves/:id/approve  (verb in URL)
fastify.post('/api/leaves/:id/approve', async (request, reply) => {
  const leave = await prisma.leave.update({
    where: { id: request.params.id },
    data: {
      status: 'approved',
      approvedBy: request.user.id,
      approvedAt: new Date()
    }
  });
  // Emit Socket.io notification
  io.to(`user:${leave.userId}`).emit('leave:approved', leave);
  reply.send(leave);
});

// Pagination: Link header (strict) vs query param (pragmatic)
// Strict: Link: ; rel="next"
// Pragmatic: { data: [...], meta: { page: 1, total: 50, hasNext: true } }

5. Debugging Narrative — HR Leave System

Symptom: The Angular frontend team complained that they couldn't cache leave approval responses. Every time a manager approved a leave, the dashboard required a full reload to show the updated status.

Root Cause: The approval endpoint was POST /api/leaves/:id/approve. POST responses are not cacheable by default, and the browser didn't invalidate the GET cache for /api/leaves/:id when a POST to a different URL (.../approve) succeeded.

Fix: Manoj changed the endpoint to PATCH /api/leaves/:id with { status: 'approved' } in the body. PATCH to the same resource URL caused the browser to invalidate the cached GET response for that URL. The frontend could then re-fetch and get fresh data without a full reload.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Verb-based URLs break HTTP cachingUse resource URLs with method + body to express actions
Over-engineering HATEOAS for internal APIsUse HATEOAS only for public/partner APIs
Nested resources too deep (/a/:id/b/:id/c)Flatten to max 2 levels; use query params for filtering
Inconsistent naming (plural vs singular)Pick a convention (plural: /contacts) and enforce via linter

7. Validation Strategy

  • Manoj's choice: Pragmatic REST for internal team APIs, stricter REST for ContactsHub's public-facing API
  • Contract test: Use OpenAPI spec + Prism mock server to validate compliance
  • Cache test: Verify GET responses include correct Cache-Control and ETag headers
  • Naming audit: Lint all route definitions for consistent plural nouns and no verbs

Designing REST Constraints Implementation

1. Definition & Context

Designing a RESTful API for a mid-size product means applying Fielding's constraints practically: stateless requests (each request contains all needed context via JWT), resource-oriented URLs (nouns not verbs), proper HTTP methods (GET=read, POST=create, PUT=replace, PATCH=partial update, DELETE=remove), cacheability (ETags, Cache-Control), and a uniform interface (consistent error format, pagination, filtering).

The design challenge is balancing purity with productivity. A practical REST API needs conventions for: versioning (URL path vs header), pagination (cursor vs offset), filtering (query params), error format (RFC 7807 Problem Details), and bulk operations.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, he designed the API with these REST conventions: /api/v1/leaves (versioned), cursor-based pagination for the leave history (employees could have hundreds of leave records), query parameter filtering (?status=pending&type=sick&from=2025-01-01), and RFC 7807 error responses. The API was consumed by the Angular 18 frontend and a future mobile app, so consistency was critical.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// Fastify REST API with conventions (HR Leave System)
import Fastify from 'fastify';

const app = Fastify();

// Standardized response envelope
interface ApiResponse<T> {
  data: T;
  meta?: { cursor?: string; hasMore: boolean; total?: number };
}

// Cursor-based pagination
app.get('/api/v1/leaves', async (request, reply) => {
  const { cursor, limit = 20, status, type } = request.query;
  const where: any = {};
  if (status) where.status = status;
  if (type) where.leaveType = type;
  if (cursor) where.id = { gt: parseInt(cursor) };

  const leaves = await prisma.leave.findMany({
    where,
    take: Number(limit) + 1, // Fetch one extra to check hasMore
    orderBy: { id: 'asc' },
    include: { user: { select: { name: true } } }
  });

  const hasMore = leaves.length > Number(limit);
  if (hasMore) leaves.pop();

  const response: ApiResponse = {
    data: leaves,
    meta: {
      cursor: leaves.at(-1)?.id?.toString(),
      hasMore
    }
  };

  reply
    .header('Cache-Control', 'private, max-age=30')
    .send(response);
});

// RFC 7807 error handler
app.setErrorHandler((error, request, reply) => {
  reply.code(error.statusCode || 500).send({
    type: `https://api.hrlms.com/errors/${error.code || 'internal'}`,
    title: error.message,
    status: error.statusCode || 500,
    detail: error.detail || undefined,
    instance: request.url
  });
});

5. Debugging Narrative — HR Leave System

Symptom: The Angular frontend's infinite scroll for leave history occasionally skipped records or showed duplicates when a new leave was created while the user was scrolling.

Investigation: Manoj reproduced the issue by creating a new leave while the list was paginated to page 3 (offset-based). The new leave inserted at the beginning shifted all records, causing page 4 to repeat the last record from page 3.

Root Cause: Offset-based pagination is unstable when data is inserted or deleted during pagination. OFFSET 40 LIMIT 20 returns different results if a row is inserted before position 40.

Fix: Manoj switched from offset-based to cursor-based pagination using the leave ID as cursor. WHERE id > cursor ORDER BY id ASC LIMIT 20 is stable regardless of insertions because the cursor points to a specific record, not a position.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Offset pagination skips/duplicates on mutationUse cursor-based pagination for mutable lists
API versioning proliferation (v1, v2, v3...)Use additive changes; only bump version for breaking changes
Inconsistent error formats across endpointsUse centralized error handler with RFC 7807 format
Over-fetching nested relationsUse ?include=user,department query param for sparse fields

7. Validation Strategy

  • OpenAPI spec: Define all endpoints in OpenAPI 3.0; auto-generate client SDKs
  • Contract test: Validate responses against JSON Schema in CI
  • Pagination test: Insert/delete during pagination — verify no skips or duplicates
  • Error format test: Assert all 4xx/5xx responses match RFC 7807 structure

Designing a CAP Theorem Implementation

1. Definition & Context

The CAP theorem states that a distributed system can only guarantee two of three properties: Consistency (every read returns the most recent write), Availability (every request receives a response), and Partition tolerance (the system continues operating despite network partitions). Since network partitions are unavoidable in distributed systems, the real choice is between CP (consistent but may reject requests during partitions) and AP (available but may return stale data).

In practice, the choice is per-operation, not per-system. A payment system needs CP for balance checks but can be AP for transaction history display. Modern databases offer tunable consistency: MongoDB's read/write concerns, PostgreSQL's synchronous replication.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave System

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, different features required different CAP trade-offs. The leave balance was a CP concern: if a user had 5 days remaining and submitted a 5-day leave, the system must never approve it twice (which would over-deduct). Manoj used PostgreSQL with serializable transactions for balance operations. The notification feed (Socket.io messages) was an AP concern: it was acceptable if a notification appeared 2 seconds late during network issues, but unacceptable if the notification system blocked the entire leave approval flow.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// CP: Serializable transaction for leave balance (PostgreSQL + Prisma)
async function approveLeave(leaveId: string, approverId: string) {
  return prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
    // Lock the balance row — CP: consistency over availability
    const balance = await tx.$queryRaw`
      SELECT * FROM leave_balances 
      WHERE user_id = ${userId} 
      FOR UPDATE  -- Row-level lock prevents concurrent reads
    `;
    
    const leave = await tx.leave.findUnique({ where: { id: leaveId } });
    if (!leave) throw new Error('Leave not found');
    
    const requestedDays = calculateBusinessDays(leave.startDate, leave.endDate);
    if (balance.remaining < requestedDays) {
      throw new Error(`Insufficient balance: ${balance.remaining} < ${requestedDays}`);
    }
    
    // Atomically update both tables
    await tx.leaveBalance.update({
      where: { userId: leave.userId },
      data: { remaining: { decrement: requestedDays } }
    });
    
    return tx.leave.update({
      where: { id: leaveId },
      data: { status: 'approved', approvedBy: approverId }
    });
  }, {
    isolationLevel: 'Serializable' // Strongest consistency
  });
}

// AP: Notifications can be eventually consistent
async function notifyLeaveApproval(leave: Leave) {
  try {
    io.to(`user:${leave.userId}`).emit('leave:approved', leave);
  } catch (error) {
    // AP: Don't fail the approval if notification fails
    // Queue for retry instead
    await notificationQueue.add('leave:approved', leave);
  }
}

5. Debugging Narrative — HR Leave System

Symptom: Two managers simultaneously approved the same employee's leave request. The employee's balance went negative (-3 days), which should be impossible.

Investigation: Manoj checked the transaction logs and found both approval transactions ran at the Read Committed isolation level (Prisma default). Both read the balance as 5 days remaining before either had committed the decrement.

Root Cause: Read Committed allows concurrent transactions to read the same value before either commits. Both transactions saw balance=5 and approved, decrementing to -3.

Fix: Manoj upgraded to Serializable isolation with SELECT ... FOR UPDATE to lock the balance row. The second transaction now waits for the first to commit, then reads the updated balance (0 days) and correctly rejects the second approval with a 409 Conflict response.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Using CP for everything (slow, reduces availability)Classify operations by consistency need — use AP where safe
Serializable transactions cause deadlocksKeep transactions short; use consistent lock ordering
Eventual consistency confuses users (stale reads)Show optimistic UI updates with "pending" indicators
Assuming single-node = no CAP issuesEven single-node has concurrent transactions; use proper isolation

7. Validation Strategy

  • Concurrency test: Run 10 parallel approval requests — verify balance never goes negative
  • Partition simulation: Kill Socket.io connection mid-approval — verify leave still approved
  • Deadlock test: Two users approving each other's leaves simultaneously
  • Recovery test: Restart server mid-transaction — verify no partial commits

Debugging CAP Theorem Issues

1. Definition & Context

CAP-related bugs manifest as data inconsistency, lost updates, or phantom reads in distributed or concurrent systems. Debugging requires understanding the consistency guarantees of your database and identifying where concurrent operations violate expectations. Common symptoms: duplicate records, negative balances, stale reads after writes, and "lost update" anomalies.

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, the app used MongoDB for contact storage. When two users edited the same contact simultaneously (one updating the phone, the other updating the email), the last write won — overwriting the first user's change entirely. This was a classic lost update anomaly caused by MongoDB's default read/write concern and the app using full-document replacement instead of field-level updates.

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Code Example

// Bug: Full document replacement causes lost updates
// ContactsHub — Mongoose
app.patch('/api/contacts/:id', async (req, res) => {
  // BAD: replaceOne overwrites everything
  // const result = await Contact.replaceOne({ _id: req.params.id }, req.body);
  
  // GOOD: Use $set for field-level updates
  const result = await Contact.updateOne(
    { _id: req.params.id },
    { $set: req.body }  // Only updates provided fields
  );
  
  // BETTER: Optimistic concurrency with version field
  const result = await Contact.findOneAndUpdate(
    { _id: req.params.id, __v: req.body.__v },  // Version check
    { $set: req.body, $inc: { __v: 1 } },
    { new: true }
  );
  
  if (!result) {
    return res.status(409).json({
      error: 'Conflict',
      message: 'Contact was modified by another user. Please refresh.'
    });
  }
  res.json(result);
});

5. Debugging Narrative — ContactsHub

Symptom: A user updated a contact's phone number but the email field reverted to the old value. The user was certain they hadn't changed the email.

Investigation: Manoj added request logging and found two PATCH requests hitting the server 2 seconds apart for the same contact. The first request (from User A) set email: "new@example.com". The second request (from User B, who had the page open since before A's edit) sent the entire contact object with the old email, overwriting A's change.

Root Cause: The frontend sent the entire contact object on save (read-modify-write), and the backend used replaceOne instead of $set. This meant B's stale copy overwrote A's fresh data.

Fix: Two changes: (1) Backend switched from replaceOne to updateOne with $set, so only changed fields were written. (2) Added optimistic concurrency control using Mongoose's __v (version) field — if the version didn't match, the update returned 409 Conflict and the frontend prompted the user to refresh.

6. Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Full document replacement on PATCHUse $set for partial updates, never replaceOne
No optimistic concurrency controlAdd version field (__v) and check on update
MongoDB default write concern (w:1)Use w: 'majority' for critical writes
Stale UI after concurrent editShow conflict resolution UI or auto-merge non-conflicting fields

7. Validation Strategy

  • Concurrent edit test: Two Playwright sessions editing the same contact — verify 409 on stale version
  • Field-level test: Update phone via A, update email via B — verify both changes preserved
  • Version increment test: Assert __v increments on each successful update
  • Recovery test: After 409, refresh and retry — verify success with fresh data
